blurb: Miscellanous texts relating to the early scriptures. These are mostly Chinese translations of texts similar to those found in the Pali canon

children.blurb: The Yizu jing (Arthavargīya) in two fascicles at T 198 was translated into Chinese by Zhi Qian 支謙 between the 2nd year of Huang Wu 黃武 and 2nd year of Jianxing 建興, Wu dynasty 呉 (223–253 CE). This corresponds to the Aṭṭhakavagga, the fourth of the five chapters of the Sutta Nipāta (Sn 766–975) of the Pāli Tipitaka, though this Chinese version has additional passages. Since the Pāli version is cited by other Pāli and Sanskrit canonical texts, traditionally the Aṭṭhaka vagga was taken to be an important encapsulation of key teachings, while scholars consider it one of the earliest compositions included in the Tipitaka. Its continuing influence amongst Buddhists is illustrated by the fact that noteworthy later citations of the text include Asaṅga in the Bodhisattvabhūmi and Vasubandhu in the Abhidharmakośa-bhāṣya.

children.translated_name: Past Life Stories of the Buddha
children.blurb: Texts that have been classified as Jātakas (本緣部), or stories of the Buddha’s past lives, similar to the Jātakas of the Pali Khuddhaka Nikāya. In the Taishō edition of the Chinese canon they are found in volumes three and four.

The Nāgasena Bhikṣu Sūtra (那先比丘經) is a Chinese version of the text found in Pali as the Milinda Pañha. The translator and date are unknown, but it is listed in the Tung Chin lu (東晉錄) (CE 317–420). In the Taishō edition of the Chinese canon, it is text number 1670B, located at T vol. 32, 703c.

This portion of the Taishō edition of the Chinese canon is simply called “miscellaneous collection” ( 經集部), and it includes sūtra texts that are not readily classifiable elsewhere. While the section is mostly for Mahāyāna sūtras, a number of parallels with early discourses have been identified.

This section includes texts recited as protection mantras, similar to the parittas of the Pali canon. Of the many texts classified in this way in the Chinese canon, two have been identified as parallel to the Pali Bhaddekaratta and Āṭānāṭiya suttas.

This section includes sutta parallels that have been identified within the portions of the Chinese canon containing treatises on Abhidharma and Mahāyāna philosophy. The identified parallels are quotations within commentarial texts.
